name: Sotho
description: The Basotho of South Africa are closely related to the people of Lesotho, whose kingdom was founded by King Moshoeshoe I in the early 19th century. He famously gathered refugees from the Mfecane wars and created a new nation on the mountain fortress of Thaba Bosiu, resisting both Zulu expansion and Boer encroachment. The Basotho blanket — a thick, colourful woollen blanket worn in all weather — is one of Africa's most iconic garments, adopted from European trade goods and completely absorbed into Sotho identity.
cultural_highlights: Basotho blanket — a heavy woollen blanket with specific patterns worn by all ages and genders; different patterns mark life stages and status
Litema wall art — geometric mural paintings applied to house walls by Basotho women using earth pigments and finger-drawing techniques
Mokorotlo (conical hat) — the woven grass hat appearing on Lesotho's flag; a daily garment turned national symbol
King Moshoeshoe's Thaba Bosiu fortress — a mountain stronghold from which he unified refugees into the Lesotho nation

food_traditions.name: Papa (maize porridge)
food_traditions.description: Thick maize meal porridge eaten with vegetables, beans, or dried meat — the daily staple of Basotho households

food_traditions.name: Merogo (wild leafy greens)
food_traditions.description: Foraged indigenous greens cooked with onion and tomato — a nutrition-rich addition to the daily meal

food_traditions.name: Sorghum beer (joala ba sesotho)
food_traditions.description: Traditionally brewed sorghum beer served in communal pots at celebrations and social gatherings

music_traditions: Sesotho folk songs (difela) — narrative poems and songs sung at weddings, funerals, and community gatherings
Lekolulo flute — a long shepherd's flute played by young men herding cattle in the mountains
Famo music — accordion-based urban genre originating in Johannesburg mines; expressive, competitive, and sometimes associated with gang culture
